summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier complaint about seeing an Apple Arcade promotion in iOS Settings despite already paying for Apple One. After communicating with people familiar with the matter, he learned the issue was a bug caused by his split Apple ID accounts — one for iCloud (dating back to mac.com in 2000) and another for iTunes purchases (from 2003). The bug affected a narrow set of users with split accounts under specific circumstances, not most Apple One subscribers. He shares the historical context of how long-time Mac users ended up with split accounts and notes that many Apple employees are in the same boat. Apple has acknowledged the bug and a fix is forthcoming in a future iOS update.
key_points: The iOS Settings promotion for Apple Arcade shown to an existing subscriber was a bug specific to split Apple ID accounts, not intentional adware behavior
Split Apple IDs are a legacy artifact common among long-time Mac users who created separate iCloud and iTunes accounts years apart, including many Apple employees
Apple has never provided a way to migrate purchases between Apple IDs, which is why split accounts persist decades later
Despite the complexity of split accounts, Apple's backend has managed to make family sharing and Apple One work across two linked accounts for years
money_quotes: I'm still not sure about this whole .Mac thing, but I'm pretty sure I'll have my daringfireball.net address forever.
In hindsight, this was a mistake on my part. Everything is simpler if you only have one Apple ID that you use for both iCloud and iTunes/App Store purchases.
The reason I never switched to using my mac.com iCloud account for everything, including purchases, is that there's never been a way to migrate old purchases from a different account.
It helps quite a bit that there are a lot of people working at Apple in the same situation.
Using split accounts has always been a bit weird, occasionally problematic and/or confusing — but has mostly just worked out.
No joke, I've purchased 744 movies (and counting) from iTunes over the last two decades. Money runs through my fingers like water.
key_insight: What appeared to be Apple aggressively promoting its own services in iOS Settings turned out to be a narrow bug affecting users with legacy split Apple ID accounts — a relic of Apple's two-decade evolution of identity services.
